item.title: Electricity Bill Program in Java
item.author: khushi
item.content:encoded: Whether you're just starting out with Java or you're an experienced developer brushing up on core concepts, the Electricity Bill Program in Java offers a clean, practical exercise in logic building, input handling, and real-world problem solving. This Java-based console application calculates the electricity bill based on the number of units consumed. It uses a slab-wise tariff system (just like utility companies do), applying different rates based on usage tiers. This project is ideal for:🧑‍🎓 Final year students preparing academic submissions👩‍💻 Java beginners learning control structures and logic🛠️ Experienced developers who enjoy simple logic-building challenges📚 Trainers or mentors teaching real-world Java examples ** Accepts input for units consumedCalculates total bill using real billing slabsEasy to extend with GUI or file/database supportClean, readable Java code When you download the project, you get:✅ Step-by-step instructions🌐 More from Projects for EveryoneLooking for similar real-world Java projects? The Electricity Bill Program in Java is a versatile project that suits everyone — from learners to experienced devs. It helps sharpen logic-building skills while demonstrating how code can model real-life billing systems.

item.title: Ned Batchelder: PyCon summer camp
item.content:encoded: I’m headed to PyCon today, and I’m reminded about how it feels like summer camp, in mostly good ways, but also in a tricky way.You take some time off from your “real” life, you go somewhere else, you hang out with old friends and meet some new friends. You do different things than in your real life, some are playful, some take real work. These are all good ways it’s like summer camp.Here’s the tricky thing to watch out for: like summer camp, you can make connections to people or projects that are intense and feel like they could last forever. You make friends at summer camp, or even have semi-romantic crushes on people. You promise to stay in touch, you think it’s the “real thing.” When you get home, you write an email or two, maybe a phone call, but it fades away. The excitement of the summer is overtaken by your autumnal real life again.PyCon can be the same way, either with people or projects. Not a romance, but the exciting feeling that you want to keep doing the project you started at PyCon, or be a member of some community you hung out with for those days. You want to keep talking about that exciting thing with that person. These are great feelings, but it’s easy to emotionally over-commit to those efforts and then have it fade away once PyCon is over.How do you know what projects are just crushes, and which are permanent relationships? Maybe it doesn’t matter, and we should just get excited about things.I know I started at least one effort last year that I thought would be done in a few months, but has since stalled. Now I am headed back to PyCon. Will I become attached to yet more things this time? Is that bad? Should I temper my enthusiasm, or is it fine to light a few fires and accept that some will peter out?

item.title: Top Debt Recovery Strategies for Small Business Owners
item.author: VIP Arabian Safari
item.content:encoded: As a small business owner, cash flow is one of the most critical aspects of your business’s success. When clients delay or fail to make payments, it can put immense pressure on your operations, impacting everything from paying bills to investing in growth. Unfortunately, debt is a common issue, and recovering it can be one of the most challenging tasks a business owner faces. However, handling small business debt recovery doesn’t have to be painful or confrontational. By employing the right strategies, you can recover debts while maintaining positive client relationships and ensuring business continuity.In this guide, we will explore the most effective debt recovery strategies small business owners can adopt. These approaches will help ensure that overdue payments are collected in a professional, systematic, and respectful manner.Establish Clear Payment Terms Upfront The best way to avoid debt-related issues in the future is to prevent them from happening in the first place. This begins with clear and transparent payment terms established before any work or transaction begins. Steps to Take: Set Clear Payment Terms in Contracts: Always include the payment due date, penalties for late payments, and payment methods in your contracts. Specify whether you expect full payment upon delivery or partial payments with agreed-upon milestones.Discuss Terms with Clients: Before you begin, have a conversation with clients about the terms, ensuring that they are on board with the payment schedule and penalties.Use Detailed Invoices: Provide clients with invoices that are clear and specific, showing exactly what they are being charged for, the due date, and the payment options.By setting expectations from the start, you significantly reduce the chances of confusion or disputes down the road.Invoice Promptly and Professionally Once the work is completed or the products are delivered, sending out an invoice promptly is key to initiating the payment process. Delayed invoices can confuse clients or make them feel that the payment deadline is flexible. Tips for Professional Invoicing: Send Invoices Immediately: The moment the work is complete or the product is shipped, send the invoice. This ensures that clients are aware of the charges and due dates.Use Online Invoicing Software: Consider using tools like QuickBooks, FreshBooks, or Zoho, which automate the invoicing process and allow you to track the status of your invoices.Be Clear and Accurate: Make sure your invoices include all necessary information, such as client name, your business name, details of the work performed, the total amount due, payment instructions, and the due date.A well-organized invoice not only aids the payment process but also reflects your professionalism, which can positively impact your client relationship.Send Friendly Payment Reminders Sometimes clients simply forget or overlook payments, and a friendly reminder can encourage them to settle their debts. Regular follow-ups are essential to maintaining cash flow without damaging client relationships. How to Follow Up: Send a Reminder Before the Due Date: A gentle reminder a few days before the payment due date can ensure that clients are prepared to pay on time. A simple “heads up” email or message is effective.Follow Up Immediately After the Due Date: If a payment is missed, send a polite email thanking the client for their business and gently reminding them of the overdue invoice. Include a copy of the invoice in case it was misplaced.Use Multiple Channels: Some clients may prefer emails, while others may respond better to phone calls. Don’t hesitate to use different communication channels (email, phone calls, text messages, etc.) to remind clients.Timing and tone are crucial. Keep the message respectful and avoid being confrontational, as it can damage the relationship.Offer Payment Plans or Installments Some clients may struggle with making a lump sum payment due to their own cash flow issues. Offering them a payment plan can not only increase the likelihood of getting paid, but it also shows that you care about their business and are willing to be flexible. How to Structure Payment Plans: Offer Installments: Let the client pay in smaller amounts over a set period. For example, they might pay 25% of the balance every 30 days until the full amount is paid.Negotiate Terms: Tailor payment plans based on the client’s financial situation, but ensure you don’t extend the terms too far out. Set clear expectations about the timing and interest (if any).Get It in Writing: Always confirm payment arrangements in writing to prevent any confusion or future misunderstandings.A payment plan can be a win-win: you recover the debt, and your client doesn’t feel overwhelmed by a large upfront payment.Implement Late Payment Fees Late payment fees are an effective way to encourage clients to settle debts quickly. When clients are aware that delays will result in added costs, they are more likely to prioritize their payments. Guidelines for Late Fees: Include Late Fee Clauses in Contracts: Ensure that clients are aware of late payment penalties before the work begins. These fees should be clearly mentioned in your service agreements or contracts.Be Reasonable: Late fees should be reasonable—typically 1% to 5% of the total invoice amount per month. A small fee will encourage clients to pay on time without creating unnecessary tension.Give a Grace Period: Offering a short grace period (5–10 days) before late fees are applied can show flexibility, while still incentivizing timely payments.Late fees are a reminder that paying on time has financial benefits and that delays can be costly.Seek Mediation Before Legal Action In cases where clients consistently fail to pay and you cannot reach an agreement through your standard follow-up methods, mediation can provide an alternative solution to legal action. Mediation is often less expensive, quicker, and preserves client relationships. Steps to Take: Suggest Third-Party Mediation: A neutral third-party mediator can help both parties reach an agreement without resorting to costly litigation. Mediation is often less formal and less adversarial.Provide Evidence: Gather all relevant documentation (contracts, invoices, communication logs) to present to the mediator.Be Open to Compromise: Sometimes, clients may offer a reasonable settlement to avoid further escalation. Be open to negotiating.Mediation allows both sides to express their concerns and reach a mutually beneficial resolution without damaging the business relationship.Hire a Debt Collection Agency When all else fails, and the debt remains unpaid despite your best efforts, it may be time to involve a professional debt collection agency. However, this step should not be taken lightly as it may damage the client relationship. It’s important to choose an agency that specializes in small business debt recovery while maintaining professionalism. How to Choose a Debt Collection Agency: Research the Agency’s Reputation: Choose an agency with a solid track record of recovering debts for small businesses without damaging reputations.Ensure They Are Transparent: The agency should be clear about their fees, which are typically a percentage of the recovered debt.Review Their Approach: Choose an agency that uses a “soft” collection approach, which focuses on customer service and maintaining relationships.Although hiring a debt collection agency is more formal and final, it may be necessary to recover significant debts while minimizing your time and effort.Take Legal Action if Necessary As a last resort, small business owners can pursue legal action to recover debts. This can be through small claims court or by hiring an attorney for larger debts. While this route can be expensive and time-consuming, it may be necessary in some cases. Legal Considerations: Consider Small Claims Court: For smaller amounts (typically under $10,000), small claims court is an accessible and low-cost way to recover debts without hiring an attorney.Consult an Attorney: If the debt is significant, an attorney specializing in commercial debt recovery can guide you through the legal process.Know the Risks: Legal action can damage client relationships permanently and may not always result in a favorable outcome.Legal action should always be your final resort. Weigh the potential benefits and the likelihood of recovery before pursuing this route.Learn From the Experience Every unpaid debt is an opportunity to learn and improve your processes. Once the debt is recovered (or not), take a moment to evaluate what went wrong and what can be done better in the future. What to Reflect On: Were your payment terms clear enough?Could you have communicated better with the client?Was the creditworthiness of the client properly assessed before offering services?By analyzing what happened, you can make adjustments to prevent future issues.Small business debt recovery doesn’t have to be an adversarial or stressful process. With the right strategies in place, you can recover owed money while maintaining strong client relationships. Start by setting clear expectations, follow up regularly, and offer flexible payment options. If necessary, involve professionals, but always maintain a respectful approach.By focusing on proactive measures and communication, you can prevent most debt issues from arising and handle those that do with confidence and professionalism. Remember, a well-managed debt recovery process strengthens your business's financial health and reputation.

item.title: How to Build AI Agents Using Python: A Complete Guide
item.author: AI Development Company
item.content:encoded: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become a cornerstone of innovation across industries, and AI agents are at the heart of many intelligent systems. These agents are capable of perceiving their environment, reasoning, learning, and taking actions to achieve specific goals. Python, with its simplicity and vast ecosystem of libraries, is the go-to language for AI agent development.In this article, we’ll dive into how to build AI agents using Python, covering the fundamentals, tools, libraries, and best practices. Whether you’re a developer, startup founder, or part of an AI development company, this guide will walk you through every essential step of the process. An AI agent is a software entity that autonomously interacts with its environment to achieve specific goals. It perceives its surroundings, processes information, and takes actions to influence future states.Real-World Examples of AI Agents: Chatbots providing customer supportVirtual assistants like Alexa or SiriGame bots that learn and adapt strategiesSelf-driving car control systemsAI agent development is about creating systems that are intelligent, adaptive, and capable of decision-making—often powered by Python-based machine learning models.Why Use Python to Build AI Agents? Python has emerged as the leading programming language in the AI world for several reasons: Easy syntax allows developers to focus on problem-solving rather than complex coding structures. Rich ecosystem for AI and data science (TensorFlow, PyTorch, OpenAI Gym, Scikit-learn). A vast and active developer community offering solutions, tutorials, and tools.Cross-Platform Integration: Seamless integration with web apps, APIs, databases, and IoT systems.These strengths make Python ideal for AI agent development, from academic prototypes to full-scale enterprise applications.Types of AI Agents You Can Build with Python Before learning how to build AI agents, it’s important to understand the different types: Use condition-action rules.Model-Based Reflex Agents: Maintain internal states to make informed decisions. Aim to achieve specific objectives. Evaluate multiple goals and choose the most beneficial. Use machine learning to improve performance over time.Python can be used to build all of these agent types, making it a flexible choice for both beginners and experts.How to Build AI Agent Using Python: Step-by-Step Now let’s walk through the step-by-step process of building an AI agent using Python.Step 1: Define the Agent's Objective Start by identifying the purpose of your AI agent.What problem will it solve?What environment will it operate in?What actions should it take?Example: Build a chatbot that answers medical questions using natural language understanding.Step 2: Set Up the Environment You’ll need a Python development environment. Recommended tools include:Jupyter Notebook or VS CodeVirtualenv or Conda for managing dependenciesInstall essential libraries:pip install numpy pandas scikit-learn tensorflow gymThese libraries cover everything from data handling to machine learning and reinforcement learning.Step 3: Choose an Agent Architecture Pick the type of AI agent based on your use case.For simple automation: reflex agentFor dynamic environments: model-based agentFor decision-making: goal or utility-basedFor learning and adapting: reinforcement learning agentFor example, OpenAI’s Gym is great for creating agents that learn in simulated environments.Step 4: Develop the Perception System Your AI agent must first perceive its environment through inputs or sensors.In Python, this could mean:Parsing user input (for chatbots)Processing sensor data (for IoT agents)Reading environment states (in reinforcement learning)NLTK / spaCy for language understandingSensors or APIs for real-world dataStep 5: Build the Decision-Making Logic This is the “brain” of the agent.You can implement logic using:Rule-based systems (if...else, decision trees)Search algorithms (A*, BFS for pathfinding)Machine learning models (classification, regression)Reinforcement learning (Q-learning, DQN)Example of simple rule-based logic:def agent_action(input): if input == "hungry": elif input == "tired": else:For advanced use cases, consider deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow or PyTorch.Step 6: Add Learning Capabilities To make your AI agent smarter over time, integrate machine learning or reinforcement learning.Example: Training a learning agent with Q-learningimport numpy as np q_table = np.zeros([state_space, action_space]) for episode in range(1000): state = env.reset() while not done: action = np.argmax(q_table[state]) new_state, reward, done, _ = env.step(action) q_table[state, action] = reward + 0.9 * np.max(q_table[new_state]) state = new_stateLearning agents can improve based on interactions, making them ideal for dynamic or unpredictable environments. Testing your AI agent is crucial for improving accuracy and reliability.Use test cases and simulationsEvaluate performance with metrics (accuracy, reward, loss)Adjust parameters and retrain modelsGet user feedback for interactive agentsAI agents must be resilient to changes in data and environment, so continuous testing is essential.Step 8: Deploy Your AI Agent Once your agent is working as expected, you can deploy it using:Flask/FastAPI for API integrationDocker for containerizationCloud platforms like AWS, Google Cloud, or AzureStreamlit or Gradio for building interactive UIsIf you're targeting production-level deployment, consider hiring an AI development company with DevOps and MLOps expertise.Real-World Applications of Python-Based AI Agents Here are some domains where Python-built AI agents are making a difference:Healthcare Virtual assistants, diagnosis predictionFinance Fraud detection, trading botsRetail Product recommendation, customer service botsGaming Adaptive non-player characters (NPCs)Manufacturing Predictive maintenance, robotic controlThese real-world implementations underscore the potential of AI agent development using Python.Should You Work With an AI Development Company?Building AI agents requires expertise in data science, machine learning, software architecture, and deployment. If your team lacks experience or you're aiming for enterprise-scale deployment, it’s wise to partner with a professional AI development company.Access to experienced AI engineersScalable and secure infrastructurePost-launch support and maintenanceChoosing the right partner can help bring your AI vision to life while minimizing risks.Understanding how to build AI agents using Python empowers developers and organizations to create powerful, intelligent systems across domains. Python’s extensive libraries, ease of use, and community support make it the best language for AI agent development, from concept to deployment.Whether you're creating a smart chatbot, a game-playing agent, or an intelligent automation tool, Python gives you the tools you need. And if you're scaling fast or working in a regulated space, don’t hesitate to partner with a trusted AI development company to streamline the journey.

item.title: How AI Is Transforming Telegram Bot Development
item.author: Ivan Lysak
item.content:encoded: Over the past few years, Telegram bots have evolved from simple, rule-based scripts into powerful, context-aware assistants—thanks largely to the advent of accessible AI models and NLP APIs. Developers can now integrate features like natural-language understanding, sentiment analysis, and dynamic content generation without managing complex infrastructure or training data from scratch.This shift means bots are no longer limited to static command menus or keyword triggers; they can hold fluid conversations, personalize user interactions, and even learn from ongoing chats to improve over time. As a result, projects that once took weeks to spin up can now launch in days with far richer functionality.What impact has AI had on your Telegram bot workflows, and which AI-powered features are you most excited to explore next?

item.title: Why You Should Use Python — No Bullshit
item.author: Velvosoft
item.content:encoded: Not a big fan of Python Language. But once, I asked my professor, "Why should we use Python? Every day, new programming languages are emerging—and many of them are top-notch." I also added, "Honestly, I think Python might die out in the future." Then, The Professor smiled and said "Ohh, you kiddie! Programming languages don’t really die—they evolve, adapt, or sometimes fade in popularity. It's not hard to understand why Python is incredibly popular today. Why? Because it’s versatile, easy to learn, and backed by a massive ecosystem." So, Let’s cut to the chase. And Let me give you a brief idea that Why You Should Use Python just to make your life easier and it's not bullshit. Python’s syntax is clean and straightforward. It looks almost like plain English, so you don’t waste time deciphering complicated code. This means faster development and fewer headaches when you come back to your own code weeks later or when someone else has to maintain it.Whether you want to build a quick script to automate a boring task, a web application with Flask or Django, or dive into data science, AI and machine learning, Python’s got you covered. From startups to giant tech companies, Python’s used everywhere.You don’t have to reinvent the wheel. Python has thousands of pre-built libraries and frameworks; think Django for web, TensorFlow for AI, Pandas for data, and many more. Whatever you want to do, someone’s already built tools to make it easier.When you hit a wall, help is just a Google search away. Python has one of the largest and most active programming communities. Tutorials, forums, Stack Overflow answers everything is readily available.Python is perfect if you’re just starting out because it’s simple, but it’s also powerful enough for experts working on complex projects. This means you can start small and grow without switching languages later.If you want to get shit done without wasting time on syntax headaches or building everything from scratch, Python is your best bet. It’s fast to write, easy to maintain, and insanely powerful. End of story.

item.title: I Replaced Bash Scripts With Python One-Liners
item.author: Snappy Tuts
item.content:encoded:
🎁 Instant download. One-time price (less than a single coffee).Bash is powerful… until it’s not. After years of writing quick shell scripts to automate tasks, I found myself constantly debugging cryptic syntax errors, escaping characters like a magician, and rewriting the same utilities over and over. That’s when I started replacing entire Bash scripts with Python one-liners—and it changed everything.In this post, I’ll show you how and why you might want to make the switch too.
🧩 The Problem With Bash: Readability and Reusability But even a simple script like this:file .txt errors.log Can become a nightmare once you:Handle special charactersTry to scale it for reuseNow compare that to Python 👇
🐍 The Python One-Liner Equivalent python3 Yep. One line. Readable. Expandable. And debug-friendly.Need something more powerful? You can just move it into a file. No need to rewrite the whole thing.python3 numbers.txt python3
⚙️ When to Stick With Bash Python isn’t a silver bullet. Stick with Bash when:You need to glue CLI tools quicklyYou're in a restricted environmentSimplicity outweighs readabilityIf you're writing anything more than 5 lines in Bash… try Python instead. It gives you:More libraries and structureBonus: You already know Python. Why not use it more effectively?sed 's/life/happiness/g' | awk '{print $0}' > /dev/null Meanwhile, I’m over here with:😵 Me debugging Bash scripts🧘‍♂️ Me writing Python one-liners💡 When I discovered 🏆 When my cronjob actually ran successfullyWant more one-liner recipes? Drop a 💥 in the comments and I’ll do a Part 2.

item.title: 🔧 Monitoring Server Health with a Python Script That Runs Every Minute
item.author: Emmanuel Onesime
item.content:encoded: Introduction In any production environment, monitoring server performance is essential for maintaining availability and preventing system failures. For system administrators, real-time information about memory, CPU, and disk usage is crucial to ensure that infrastructure remains healthy. In this article, we'll walk through creating a Python script that runs every minute, collects server health metrics, and sends a report via email to a system administrator. We'll also log each execution and use tail -f to monitor logs in real-time. Steps to Follow:1.1 First, create a Python virtual environment: 1.2 install the required packages:Create a .env File for Your Secrets In your home directory, create the file /home/emma/.env with the following content (replace with your actual credentials): Notice: The EMAIL_PASSWORD is your Google App Password, not your real Gmail password.Write the Python Script Create the file /home/emma/server_health_report.py with the following code:Schedule the Script to Run Every Minute 4.1 Edit your crontab: 4.2 Add the following line: ****/home/emma/env/bin/python3/home/emma/server_health_report.yMonitor the Log in Real-Time Conclusion By following these steps, you've built an automated monitoring system using Python that runs every minute, gathers key server metrics, logs the results, and sends real-time performance data via email. This setup enhances visibility and helps system administrators react promptly to any issues - improving reliability and efficiency in server management.

item.title: Never get caught off guard: Receive your server's health every minute by Email
item.author: Philippe borribo
item.content:encoded:
For system administrators, surprises are rarely a good thing. Whether it's a sudden spike in CPU usage, memory running dangerously low, or disk space nearing its limit, these issues can quickly escalate into major problems : from system slowdowns to full outages. The key to avoiding such disasters? Proactive monitoring. Imagine receiving a quick health check of your server every single minute: delivered straight to your inbox. No need to log in and manually check metrics. Instead, you stay one step ahead, spotting trouble before it affects users or services. In this article, you'll learn how to set up a simple but powerful script that runs automatically every minute. It collects key system metrics like CPU, memory, and disk usage, then emails the report to your system administrator. It's fast, lightweight, and incredibly effective - a perfect fit for anyone managing production environments or personal servers alike.1. Why monitor your server every minute?When it comes to server health, timing is everything. Even a few minutes of downtime can have serious consequences… from lost revenue to broken user trust. While traditional monitoring solutions may check system status every 5 or 10 minutes, some environments require faster feedback and immediate awareness. Monitoring your server every minute gives you near real-time visibility into its behavior. This is especially useful for detecting: Sudden CPU spikes due to rogue processes. Memory leaks that gradually degrade performance. Rapid disk consumption caused by logging errors, backups, or attacks. By checking these metrics every 60 seconds, you drastically reduce the time between a problem appearing and it being detected.b. Faster incident response Early detection means faster reaction. If you receive an email showing high CPU usage or critical memory shortage, you can investigate and act before the issue escalates into service disruption. In production environments, this can save hours of downtime and avoid emergency interventions.c. Lightweight alternative to complex monitoring tools While tools like Nagios, Zabbix, or Prometheus are powerful, they can be overkill for smaller setups or individual servers. A simple script combined with a cron job offers a minimalist approach to monitoring - no dashboards, no agents, no third-party services - just email notifications that work.d. Ideal for headless or remote servers If you're managing cloud instances, VPS setups, or physical servers in remote locations, having frequent performance reports in your inbox provides peace of mind. You're always informed, even when you're away from the terminal.2. What metrics should be tracked?To keep your server healthy and responsive, it's important to track a few key performance indicators. These metrics provide a snapshot of your system's current state and help you spot problems before they become critical. Here are the most essential metrics you should include in your monitoring script: CPU usage shows how much of your processor's capacity is being used at a given moment. High or constantly maxed-out CPU usage can indicate: Inefficient code or processes running in loops Malware or unauthorized scripts Overloaded services during peak trafficYou can retrieve this metric using commands like: Memory consumption is another critical metric. If your system runs out of RAM, it will start using swap space, which is significantly slower and can cause serious performance degradation. Running out of disk space can crash applications, break databases, or prevent logging. Your script should report: Total and used space per mounted partition Alert thresholds (e.g., warn if a partition is over 90%)d. Optional: load average The load average reflects how many processes are actively running or waiting for CPU time. It gives a broader view of overall system strain.e. Optional: Uptime and reboot detection Knowing how long your server has been running helps identify unexpected reboots or instability.Setting up an automated monitoring script that sends email reports every minute doesn't require complex infrastructure or expensive software. All you need are a few standard tools, most of which are already available on typical Linux distributions. Here's a breakdown of what you'll need and why: This guide assumes you're running a Unix-based system like Ubuntu, Debian, CentOS, or Red Hat. Most cloud instances (AWS, Azure, DigitalOcean, etc.) run some flavor of Linux. The commands and tools used are native to these environments.b. A Scripting Language (Bash or Python) You'll need a script that collects system metrics and formats them into an email message. Bash is ideal for simplicity and direct access to system commands. Python offers more flexibility, better formatting options, and error handling. Choose the one you're more comfortable with - both are excellent for the task. cron is the time-based job scheduler built into Linux. It allows you to run scripts at fixed intervals… like every minute. To edit the scheduled jobs, use:This is where you'll tell the system to execute your monitoring script regularly.d. Mail Utility (mailx, msmtp, or sendmail) To send system emails, your server needs a mail transfer agent (MTA) or a mail client that can send messages from the command line. mailx: Simple and commonly pre-installed. Often used with sendmail or postfix. msmtp: Lightweight and easy to configure with an external SMTP service (like Gmail). sendmail or postfix: Full-fledged MTAs but heavier to configure.For example, to install msmtp on Ubuntu:sudo apt update sudo apt install msmtp msmtp-mta Create a configuration file ~/.msmtprcAnd add your SMTP credentialsdefaults auth on tls on tls_trust_file /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt logfile ~/.msmtp.log account gmail host smtp.gmail.com port 587 from your.email@gmail.com user your.email@gmail.com password your_app_password account default : gmail e. A Valid Email Address for Delivery In your script, you'll specify the recipient email. The email is actively monitored. SMTP settings are correct to ensure deliverability. You whitelist your server's address, if needed, to avoid spam filters4. Writing the Monitoring ScriptNow that you know what metrics to track and which tools to use, it's time to write the core of the solution - the monitoring script. This script will collect the server's CPU, memory, and disk usage data, format it into a readable message, and send it to the system administrator via email. We'll write a simple Bash script, which is efficient and widely compatible across Linux distributions. The script will:Collect system performance metrics (CPU, RAM, Disk).Format the data into a plain-text report.Email the report to the system administrator.Optionally include a timestamp and hostname.b. Sample script (monitor.py)import psutil import socket import datetime import subprocess
# Collect system metrics def get_metrics(): hostname = socket.gethostname() cpu = psutil.cpu_percent(interval=1) ram = psutil.virtual_memory() disk = psutil.disk_usage('/') now = datetime.datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
message = f"""\ Subject: [ALERT] Server Metrics - {hostname} From: Monitoring <monitor@localhost> To: your@email.com
Date : {now} Server : {hostname}
CPU : {cpu}% used RAM : {ram.percent}% used ({round(ram.used / (1024**3), 2)} GB / {round(ram.total / (1024**3), 2)} GB) Disk : {disk.percent}% used ({round(disk.used / (1024**3), 2)} GB / {round(disk.total / (1024**3), 2)} GB) """ return message
# Send the email using msmtp def send_email(body): process = subprocess.Popen( ['msmtp', 'destination@email.com'], stdin=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE ) stdout, stderr = process.communicate(input=body.encode())
if process.returncode != 0: print("Failed to send email:", stderr.decode()) else: print("Email sent successfully.")
# Main if __name__ == "__main__": metrics = get_metrics() send_email(metrics) c. Make the Script Executable Save the script as monitor.py, then make it executable: Run it manually first to ensure it works as expected:Check the recipient inbox (or spam folder if necessary) to confirm receipt.5. Automating the Script with CrontabTo send metrics every minute:* * * * * python monitor.py Automating the monitoring of server health is not just a good practice, it's essential for maintaining system reliability, identifying performance issues early, and preventing downtime. With a simple Bash script, a few core Linux tools, and a properly configured email system, you can build a lightweight but powerful alerting mechanism that keeps your system administrator informed every minute. This approach doesn't require expensive monitoring platforms or complex dashboards. It leverages native system utilities and the flexibility of cron to deliver real-time performance snapshots directly to the sysadmin's inbox. By tracking key metrics such as CPU usage, memory consumption, and disk space, you create a proactive culture of system care rather than waiting for something to break. In environments where uptime and responsiveness matter, such a script can be the first line of defense… a quiet guardian running in the background, ensuring your infrastructure stays healthy and your admin stays informed.

item.title: How to Control an Already Opened Chrome Browser with Python?
item.author: Generatecode
item.content:encoded:
Using Python to control an already opened Chrome browser can streamline your automation tasks, allowing you to interact with the web without launching a new browser instance. Many developers have successfully utilized Selenium to automate browser actions by connecting to an existing Chrome session. However, sometimes issues can arise that may cause previously functional code to stop working. In this article, we'll explore common reasons why your code might fail and provide solutions to ensure you can regain control over your Chrome browser.Why Does the Code Stop Working?There are several reasons why your code controlling an already opened Chrome instance may stop functioning as expected:Chrome Version Compatibility: If your Chrome browser has updated to a new version, it may not be compatible with the version of Selenium or ChromeDriver you are using. Make sure both your Selenium library and Chrome web driver are updated to match your current Chrome version.: The debugging port specified in your command () must match the port your Python code is connecting to. Any discrepancy can prevent your script from connecting.: If the remote debugging features are not properly enabled or authorized, Chrome may reject connections.: If there are changes in your specific environment or the Selenium library, the way to instantiate the might behave differently.Step-by-Step Solution to Control ChromeTo control your opened Chrome browser using Python, follow these steps ensured to work with the current configurations.Step 1: Ensure Chrome is Launched in Debugging ModeBefore running your Python script, launch Chrome with the remote debugging option. Make sure you correctly enter the full path to your Chrome executable and specify the remote debugging port like this:"C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" --remote-debugging-port=9222 Step 2: Install Necessary PackagesMake sure you have the required libraries installed. You can install them using pip if you haven't done so already:Step 3: Create Your Python ScriptUse the following Python script to connect to the already opened instance of Chrome. This example will open a specific URL and interact with it:from selenium import webdriver from selenium.webdriver.chrome.service import Service from selenium.webdriver.chrome.options import Options
# Options to connect to existing Chrome session chrome_option = Options() chrome_option.add_experimental_option("debuggerAddress", "localhost:9222")
# Service is not required to provide executable_path, because we are connecting to an existing instance service = Service() # Note: No executable_path needed
drivers = webdriver.Chrome(service=service, options=chrome_option)
# Navigate to a specific URL url = "http://www.example.com" drivers.get(url)
# Now you can interact with the page # For example, let's print the title of the page print(drivers.title) Troubleshooting Common IssuesIf you follow the steps above and your script is still failing to connect, consider the points below:: If Chrome was recently updated, update your ChromeDriver. The version must match the currently installed Chrome.Verify Port Configuration: Ensure that the debugging port is still . You can verify this by checking if Chrome started correctly in debugging mode. Running the command multiple times without closing previous instances may sometimes cause conflicts.: Ensure your script has the right permissions to interact with the Chrome instance. Running the script as an administrator may help.Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)Q1: Can I use any other browser besides Chrome with Selenium? Yes, Selenium supports multiple browsers including Firefox, Safari, and Edge. However, the remote debugging feature is specific to Chrome.Q2: Is there any alternate way to control an open Chrome session? Yes, other libraries such as Pyppeteer can also automate browser tasks and are an option if you face persistent issues with Selenium in this specific use case.Q3: How can I debug issues if the code still doesn't work? Check for error messages in your console, and review the settings in both Chrome and your Python script for mismatched configurations.

item.title: What kind of “happy” is this?
item.author: UKA
item.content:encoded:
A quiet breakdown of a small Japanese word—and what I felt while writing it.
🤔 A Japanese word that’s hard to translate: “うれしい” I usually write in Japanese first, then rebuild the piece in English. It’s part of how I’m learning. So if you're reading this—thank you. Truly.One word I always struggle to translate is “うれしい” (). It’s often taught as “happy.” But that’s never quite enough.So I started wondering—what is this word really made of? Let’s break it down and see.
🤗 Some moments that feel like happiness They all count as “うれしい” in Japanese— but in English, each one seems to carry a different shade. Here are some moments that stuck with me:Possible English expressionsPosting an article on Dev.toExpression & Accomplishmentgrateful, touched, that was so thoughtfulTake these two: “Winning a prize” and “Being treated to dinner.” Both are joyful moments—but the feeling is very different, isn’t it?
😏 Code time, just for fun Breaking this down made me want to… code it. So I put together a tiny script instead:Totally impractical, but oddly satisfying. There’s something fun about mapping emotions like this.
☺️ Not a conclusion, just a little space Maybe it's not a single word at all. It might be a bundle of tiny reactions—some quiet, some loud. There’s soft happiness, and jump-up-and-down happiness. And when I try to translate it, I don’t want to lose the shape of what it really felt like.So lately, I’ve been asking myself: “What kind of happy is this?” And just holding that question already feels a little happy.I guess I don’t write to explain things perfectly— I just hope the warmth comes through. That might be my real motivation for writing. (WillVector, maybe? lol)And if something I wrote actually reaches you... then yeah— (With a smile, of course.)
🫣 A small extra: the JSON behind it all (For those curious about how it all connects—here’s the quiet logic behind it.)

item.title: Why Do Submodules Fail to Import in Pytest for Subfolders?
item.author: Generatecode
item.content:encoded:
When working with a complex test folder structure in Python, particularly with pytest, you might encounter issues with importing submodules depending on how you run your tests. This blog article will explore the problem of attribute errors related to submodules in your test directory and provide actionable solutions to run tests effectively, whether you’re targeting specific subfolders or the entire tests directory.Understanding the Folder StructureIn the provided test folder layout:tests ├── __init__.py ├── conftest.py ├── data_validation │ └── test_data_validation.py └── per_vendor ├── __init__.py ├── vendor_a │ ├── conftest.py │ └── test_vendor_a.py └── vendor_b ├── __init__.py ├── conftest.py └── test_vendor_b.py The folder serves as the main directory containing subfolders for and . The file is meant to hold fixtures that can be shared across the test files. The intention is to run tests in isolation—specifically targeting subfolders. However, as noted, an error occurs when attempting to access when tests are run in specific settings.When you run against the entire folder, Python recognizes the submodule correctly because it inspects all submodules and packages in the import context at once. However, when you run tests targeting the subfolder specifically using the command:python -m pytest tests/data_validation/ Python does not explore the submodules fully in this context, which results in an stating that module 'tests.per_vendor' has no attribute 'vendor_a'.The behavior arises from how Python handles imports and package discovery. When pytest is run in a limited scope (like a specific subfolder), it may not import unreferenced submodules. As a result, the fixtures within may end up referencing attributes that are not yet loaded in memory.Solutions to Run Tests in SubfoldersTo resolve the issue while maintaining a modular test setup, consider implementing one of the following solutions:1. Modify Your ConfigurationOne way to ensure that the relevant submodules are correctly picked up by pytest regardless of the folder you're executing in is to use a file in your directory. Here’s an example:[pytest] addopts = --rootdir=tests Setting the helps in maintaining the correct import context for all tests.2. Modify ImportsYou could adjust how you import submodules inside your . Instead of importing them directly at the top level, import them locally within the fixture function:import pytest
@pytest.fixture def database_with_data(empty_database: Engine) -> Engine: from tests.per_vendor import vendor_a, vendor_b # Local import helpers = [ vendor_a.conftest.fill_with_data_for_a, vendor_b.conftest.fill_with_data_for_b, ] for cur in helpers: cur(empty_database) return empty_database By doing this, the import is executed only when the fixture is invoked, ensuring that all necessary modules are loaded regardless of your test execution context.3. Use pytest Options or Command Line ArgumentsWhen using command-line execution to run tests, you could pass options that force pytest to load the necessary modules or specify the root directory:python -m pytest --rootdir=tests tests/data_validation/ This ensures pytest treats the folder as the starting point for module imports.4. Run All Tests But Target Specific Test CasesInstead of executing just the subfolder, consider running all tests and filtering using the option if you are targeting specific tests:python -m pytest -k "test_data_validation" This allows you to maintain the context while focusing on specific test cases.Frequently Asked QuestionsWhy do I encounter when running subfolder tests?When you run pytest in a limited scope, it doesn't import all modules fully, which may lead to missing attributes.How can I ensure all necessary modules are imported?Using a local import within your fixture or configuring pytest’s root directory in a can resolve this issue.Is there a way to run only specific tests without extraction issues?Yes, using pytest options to specify the test name or filtering can help target specific tests without losing the import context.In conclusion, understanding the structure and behavior of Python imports in combination with pytest can help resolve module import errors. By adopting proper configurations, altering import methods, or using command-line arguments effectively, you can ensure modular test organization without sacrificing the ability to run tests targeted both at the folder and file levels. Remember, a well-structured test layout not only aids in maintaining clean code but also enhances collaboration and scalability in testing practices.

item.title: Zato Blog: Using Oracle Database from Python and Zato Services
item.content:encoded: 2025-05-15, by Dariusz Suchojad Oracle Database remains a cornerstone of enterprise IT, powering mission-critical applications around the world. Integrating Oracle with Python unlocks automation, reporting, and API-based workflows. In this article, you'll learn how to:Connect to Oracle Database from PythonUse Oracle Database in Zato servicesExecute SQL queries and call stored proceduresUnderstand the underlying SQL objectsAll examples are based on real-world use cases and follow best practices for security and maintainability.Why Use Oracle Database from Python?Python is a popular language for automation, integration, and data processing. By connecting Python to Oracle Database, you can:Automate business processesBuild APIs that interact with enterprise dataRun analytics and reporting jobsIntegrate with other systems using ZatoUsing Oracle Database in Zato ServicesSQL connections are configured in the Dashboard, and you can use them directly in your service code.In all the service below, the logic is split into several dedicated services, each responsible for a specific operation. This separation improves clarity, reusability, and maintainability.Setting Up: Oracle Database ObjectsFirst, let's start with the basic SQL objects used in our examples:This service retrieves all users from the table.The service connects to Oracle using the configured connection.It executes a simple SQL query to fetch all user records.The result is returned as the service response payload.2. Querying a Specific User by IDThis service fetches a user by their using a parameterized query. There are multiple ways to retrieve results depending on whether you expect one or many rows.The service expects in the request payload.It uses a parameterized query to prevent SQL injection.The result is always a list, even if only one row matches.3. Calling a Stored Procedure with Input and Output ParametersThis service demonstrates how to call an Oracle stored procedure that takes input values and returns output values.The service prepares input and output parameters using helper classes.It calls the procedure with both input and output arguments.The result includes both output values, returned as a dictionary.Note that you always need to provide the parameters for the procedure in the same order as they were declared in the procedure itself.4. Calling a Procedure Returning Multiple RowsThis service calls a procedure that returns a set of rows (a cursor) and collects the results.The service prepares a object to receive the rows into. That is, the procedure will write rows into this object.It calls the procedure, which populates the rows.You call to get the actual rows from the database.The rows are converted to a list and returned as the payload.4. Returning a Single ObjectWhen you know your query will return a single row, you can use or for more predictable results: will return a single row as a dictionary if exactly one row is found. If no rows or more than one row are returned, it raises an exception.conn.one_or_none(query, params) will return a single row as a dictionary if one row is found, None if no rows are found, but still raises an exception if more than one row is returned.Use these methods when you expect either exactly one or zero/one results, and want to handle them cleanly. Zato handles connection pooling and configuration for you. Use self.out.sql['My Oracle DB'] to get a ready-to-use connection. Always use parameters (e.g., ) to avoid SQL injection and improve code clarity. Use helper classes (, , , , ) for input/output arguments and recordsets. Each service is focused on a single responsibility, making code easier to test and reuse.Security and Best PracticesAlways use parameterized queries for user input.Manage credentials and connection strings securely (never hardcode them in source code).Handle exceptions and database errors gracefully in production code.Use connection pooling (Zato does this for you) for efficiency.Integrating Oracle Database with Python and Zato services gives you powerful tools for building APIs, automating workflows, and connecting enterprise data sources.Whether you need to run queries, call stored procedures, or expose Oracle data through REST APIs, Zato provides a robust and Pythonic way to do it.

item.title: How to Use Redis GET Command in Python
item.author: Generatecode
item.content:encoded:
In the world of fast data retrieval, Redis shines as a powerful in-memory data structure store. When working with Redis in Python, one of the fundamental commands you'll frequently use is the GET command. This command is crucial for fetching values associated with a specific key, making it essential for efficient data handling.Understanding Redis and the GET CommandRedis, often used for caching, session management, and real-time analytics, allows developers to store data in various formats such as strings, hashes, lists, and sets. The GET command retrieves the string value associated with the specified key. If the key doesn't exist, it simply returns .Using Redis can significantly enhance the performance of your applications because it stores data in memory instead of disk, enabling faster data access. In many applications, time-sensitive operations can benefit from this rapid retrieval, simplifying architectures that would otherwise rely on slower databases.Setting Up Redis in PythonTo use Redis with Python, you first need to install the library. You can do this using pip:After installation, you can easily connect to a Redis server. Below is a simple guide to connecting and using the GET command with code examples.Connecting to Redis ServerOnce you have the Redis library installed, you can connect to the Redis server with the following steps:import redis
# Create a Redis client r = redis.StrictRedis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True) In the above code, we import the library and create a Redis client that connects to a local Redis server running on the default port 6379. The parameter ensures that returned data is a string instead of bytes.Now that you’re connected to your Redis instance, let’s see how to use the GET command. First, ensure that a value is set for a specific key.Before retrieving a value, we need to set one. Here’s how to do it:# Set a value r.set('my_key', 'Hello, Redis!') Retrieving a Value with GETNow, to fetch the value we just stored, we can use the GET command:# Get the value value = r.get('my_key') print(value) # Output: Hello, Redis! In this example, we retrieve the value associated with the key and print it. If the key does not exist, will return .Handling Non-Existent KeysIt’s good practice to handle cases where a key might not exist. You can do this by checking if the returned value is :# Attempt to retrieve a non-existent key value = r.get('non_existent_key') if value is None: print('Key does not exist.') else: print(value) This snippet would output if you try to fetch a key that hasn't been set.Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)1. What data types can I store in Redis?You can store strings, lists, sets, hashes, and more in Redis. Each type has its commands for manipulation.2. Is Redis suitable for production use?Yes, Redis is widely used in production for use cases such as caching, session storage, and real-time analytics due to its high performance and versatility.3. Can I use Redis with frameworks like Flask or Django?Absolutely! Redis is commonly used with web frameworks for session management and caching to enhance performance.In this article, we've explored how to use the Redis GET command in Python, covering everything from setting up Redis to retrieving values efficiently. The GET command is a foundational tool in Redis, enabling developers to fetch and utilize stored data quickly. By integrating Redis with your Python applications, you can enhance performance and scalability significantly. Happy coding!

item.title: Enhancing League Accuracy with Vision11 Data Scraping
item.author: DataZivot
item.content:encoded:
**Enhancing Fantasy League Accuracy with Vision11 Data Scraping and Real-Time Player Metrics
* In the dynamic realm of fantasy sports, real-time player data is a critical driver of performance analysis and predictive accuracy. Datazivot collaborated with a leading fantasy league platform to harness the power of Vision11 data scraping for actionable insights.The goal was to provide users with up-to-date player performance metrics and match intelligence, thereby improving team selection and boosting app engagement. With fantasy sports booming in India, the need for precise, scalable data solutions became imperative. The client was a fantasy sports analytics startup aiming to carve a niche in a highly competitive market. They needed robust Vision11 data scraping infrastructure to collect live match data, historical stats, and player insights. Their objective was to offer real-time updates, scoring breakdowns, and predictive suggestions through an AI-driven engine embedded in their mobile app. The client faced significant data acquisition hurdles due to the limited structure of Vision11’s platform. Real-time match data was fragmented, making manual tracking inefficient and error-prone. The lack of a public Vision11 API created further obstacles in standardizing data collection.Moreover, scaling up data scraping during live matches without triggering blocks posed a major risk. In addition, the client required enriched datasets to improve their AI predictions, which meant gathering player stats across multiple match types and formats. With rising user expectations, missing data points could compromise credibility. Finally, integrating this data with the client’s backend systems and dashboards demanded a reliable, well-structured solution built to handle high-volume loads. Datazivot deployed a customized Python Vision11 scraper capable of intelligent navigation, mimicking user behavior to avoid detection. This scraper collected comprehensive match data, player profiles, and performance metrics with high accuracy. To improve precision, a Vision11 player stats scraper module was developed, targeting both historical and live data streams. We complemented this with Sports Betting Scraping logic to analyze odds and trends, enriching the predictions engine. The system also integrated with a modular Web Scraping API for seamless delivery to the client’s analytics platform. To ensure complete coverage, we extended our solution with Mobile App Scraping to access exclusive in-app stats, and Sports & Outdoor Reviews Scraping for sentiment-based insights around player form and fan feedback. This robust approach ensured comprehensive Vision11 stats scraping, significantly enhancing user engagement and trust in the app’s predictive capabilities.* “Partnering with Datazivot gave us a strategic edge in the fantasy sports domain. Their deep expertise in Vision11 data scraping helped us unlock granular player metrics in real time, fueling our analytics engine. The integration of tools like the Vision11 API, Web Scraping API, and app-level data coverage made a real difference. Their support was seamless, and the output has directly contributed to higher user retention and trust in our predictive features.”CTO, Fantasy League Analytics Startup* This project demonstrates the power of intelligent scraping in the competitive fantasy sports space. By leveraging Vision11 data scraping and associated technologies, Datazivot enabled the client to boost accuracy, user satisfaction, and market credibility. Our end-to-end solution, from Sports betting data scraping to Vision11 stats scraping, delivered unmatched results.Ready to transform your fantasy analytics? Reach out to Datazivot for end-to-end Web Scraping API solutions built for performance.
